Right, JB in the house and I want to talk about skills training today for your daily motivation to skills morning, right? I have to get you insinuated into this idea of the power and the potential destructive ability of asking invasive questions. The biggest mistake that sales people make, especially rookies, is they ask invasive questions at the wrong time and in the wrong tonality, but especially at the wrong time. Meaning they don't go from non-invasive questions to more invasive questions. It amazed me when I was young and I saw this happen on Wall Street where like you would say to someone, you know, so imagine your first question. Interesting, this people would do this. So how much do you invest in a stock if you like it? As the first question, the guy will hang up on you 90% of the time. How much money you have in the market right now? Screw you. Click. 100% of the time. How much you have in all your brokerage accounts? They'll flip out. If you ask that question, people freak out on you. I watched in really shock and awe by the way, if they were asked three or four questions that were like sort of innocuous and non-invasive, by the fourth question, they'd really give you the social security number. It's a crazy thing. That section about invasive questions, I think it was lesson four or five in the certification, right? You have to master this art of how you ask invasive questions, the right tonalities to use and what questions need to proceed it to stop you from getting your clock clean. I want to tell you, if you break through that bubble, that invasion bubble, you know, there's like space invaders, like, you know, body language, and then there's also invasion invading the bubble through invasive questions too early. Here's a perfect example. So tell me, how long have you been living in the area for? When did you buy your home? Oh, wow, great. Oh, how long you moved? Wow. Where'd you live before that? Okay, great. Oh, and why'd you move? Oh, what, you have any kids? Real estate mortgages, right? Okay, great. Wow. Great. And right now, currently, you know, what rate are you paying on your mortgage right now? Now watch, if I say to you, what rate are you paying on your mortgage? That's a pretty, that's actually the beginning to be invasive, but it's not nearly as invasive as saying, how much money do you owe on your home? That's a personal financial question that tells me what someone's financial picture looks like. See, this is what I, this is what I need you to understand. It's like this distinction here of what really is an invasive question. What rate are you paying on your mortgage right now? That's not really an invasive question. Anyone would kind of, because why? Because I can't, what makes a question invasive is if by the prospect answering it, I know something about them that would be important through the financial health. I don't, but by someone telling me what their rate is, I really can't get much of it. I say, oh, you know, you know, right now, how much you currently have in life insurance? That's not really a fully invasive question. If you're a life insurance guy, you know, do you have life insurance right now? That's not an invasive question. If I say, what's your current equity on your policy, that's invasive. Okay. Precisely how much coverage do you have? That's invasive. Do you have coverage? That's not invasive. Now, again, you need to, whatever your industry is, so I had this thing with someone who'd come in to, I was doing stuff for, for, like a Best Buy in Australia, that their version of Best Buy, Harvey Norman, right? And there's certain questions that we wanted to find out, like, you know, are you renovating the whole house right now? What are you working on? Right? If you ask the wrong question first, you blow it, you break rapport right there. So how would you know what is an invasive question? What's a noninvasive? It's like there's this sweet spot here and what it has to do with is that ask yourself this, if someone were to answer this question honestly, would it result in me knowing something about that picture financially that they would not want me to know? That would be deemed private. It's not that a question itself is invasive or not invasive. It's does the answer result in someone's privacy being invaded? Just remember that. So it's like the question is the question. You have to move past the question and say, what will the answer provide me with? So I say, it would seem like an invasive question. It's a hardly specific question. What radio are you paying? Right? You know, how many rooms right now? Oh, tell me how many rooms you have? How much of the house are you getting redone right now? That's a specific question, but it's not invasive. I'm getting four rooms done. What does that really tell me about them? Nothing. If they're getting four rooms done. I say, what's your budget? Ah, now I'm invading them. Now I'm asking a question that they say, I know financial information about them that actually means something. What's your credit score? That's invasive. That's invasive. If I say to you, you know, oh, by the way, you know, how much you have in credit card debt? That's invasive. If I say, oh, what's your approximate rate you're paying on your credit cards right now? That's not invasive. I can't buy if I know what rate because they know, Hey, it's a great question. You know, it's going to be really high. Right. The point is if the question itself. So you would think as most would say, well, that's a very invasive question. What's your current interest rate on your credit cards? What's your mortgage rate? What rate are you getting in return right now in the bank? That's not an invasive question. It's really not. It's a little bit on the personal, but not invasive. Invasive is how much money do you have? I say to you, how's your old house? You're over a health right now. By the way, that's starting to invade. I said, how old are you right now? That's not invasive. Okay. When's the last time you were sick? That's not really invasive. How is your health right now? That's starting to get more invasive. There's something I get from it that actually gives me info that would be private. This is the reason I'm hopping on this right now is because is it, if you ask an invasive question too early, you're screwed. I promise you, you will break rapport. You will blow the deal. People do not like having their privacy invaded. And the conundrum, so to speak, is that you need to invade their privacy to do your job correctly. I want to know what someone's needs are down to the nitty gritty. I want to know what their values are. I want to know what their financial picture is. The good news is, is that you can get all that from someone. You always can pretty much. It's very rare that you can't, but you've got to work backwards.
